1:
Ps 85: Sons of Korah's Restoration Psalm — Post-Exilic Yearning. The opening verses (vv1-3) describe a past restoration in the perfect tense ('you showed favor,' 'you restored,' 'you forgave'), which most interpreters understand as the return from Babylonian exile (538 BC, under Cyrus's decree). Verses 4-7 then petition for a further restoration, suggesting the post-exilic community has returned but not fully experienced the promised renewal. The tension between 'you have restored' and 'restore us again' reflects the partially-realized eschatology of the post-exilic period: the return happened, but the full restoration described by Isaiah has not yet come.

'LORD, you were favorable to your land; you restored the fortunes of Jacob' — shavta shevit Yaakov (you turned the captivity/fortunes of Jacob) — the exile-return formula used in Jer 29:14; 30:3, 18; Ezek 39:25; Amos 9:14. The specificity 'your land' (artzecha) and 'Jacob' (Yaakov) — the covenant-patriarchal name — ground the restoration in the particular covenant relationship.
2: 'You forgave the iniquity of your people; you covered all their sin' — the forgiveness accompanying restoration: nasa avon amecha (you bore/lifted the iniquity of your people) and kissita kol-chattatam (you covered all their sin). The two forgiveness terms echo Ps 32:1-2: lifting the iniquity and covering the sin. The exile was understood as punishment for covenant sin (2 Chr 36:15-21); the restoration from exile was therefore an act of forgiveness, not merely geopolitical maneuvering.
3: 'You withdrew all your wrath; you turned from your hot anger' — asaphta kol-evratchah (you gathered in all your wrath) — the divine anger withdrawn and stored away. 'Turned from your hot anger' (shavta mecharon appechah) — the divine repentance/turning parallel to the human repentance required for restoration. In the covenantal pattern, Israel's repentance produces divine 'turning' from judgment (Jonah 3:9-10; Joel 2:13-14).
4: 'Restore us again, O God of our salvation, and put away your indignation toward us!' — the pivot to petition: despite the past restoration (vv1-3), the present situation still requires shuvenu (restore us, turn us, Qal imperative of shuv). The double meaning of shuv is crucial: restore us (bring us back from a difficult situation) and turn us (turn our hearts toward you). The same word applies to divine turning (v3: 'you turned from your anger') and human turning. Restoration and repentance are both expressed by shuv.
5: 'Will you be angry with us forever? Will you prolong your anger to all generations?' — the lament question (cf. Pss 79:5; 80:4) about the duration of divine anger. The post-exilic community's experience: the temple is rebuilt (under Zerubbabel/Haggai/Zechariah), but the full Isaianic restoration has not materialized. The Davidic throne is not restored; the nations have not flocked to Zion; the material poverty of the return is far from the abundance promised. 'Forever' and 'all generations' probe whether this partial restoration is all YHWH intends.
6: 'Will you not revive us again, that your people may rejoice in you?' — halo attah tashuv techayyenu (will you not again revive us) — the reviving (chayah in the Piel/Hiphil = give life, revive) is what the partial restoration requires: more than physical return but spiritual renewal. 'That your people may rejoice in you' (veyismach amecha bak) — joy is the test of true restoration: the returned community praying for the joy that proves the restoration is complete.
7: 'Show us your steadfast love, O LORD, and grant us your salvation' — the covenant petition: chesed (steadfast/covenant love) and yeshuah (salvation) — the twin pillars of the covenant promise. The 'showing' (areenu, show us, make visible) of chesed is the request that the invisible covenant love become experientially visible in the community's life. The salvation (yeshuah) is the yeshu'at YHWH (salvation of YHWH) that Israel's whole history has anticipated.
8: 'Let me hear what God the LORD will speak, for he will speak peace to his people, to his saints; but let them not turn back to folly' — the liturgical listening posture: the psalmist waits for the divine oracle. 'He will speak peace' (yedabber shalom) — the anticipated content of the divine word is shalom (wholeness/peace). This suggests a liturgical setting where a priest or prophet delivered an assurance-oracle (Heilsorakel) after the lament was offered. 'Let them not turn back to folly' (al-yashuvou lekesilah) — the warning embedded in the hope: divine peace cannot be claimed while returning to the covenant-foolishness that caused the exile.
9: 'Surely his salvation is near to those who fear him, that glory may dwell in our land' — the divine oracle received: salvation is qarov (near) for the fearers. 'That glory may dwell in our land' (lishkon kavod be-artzenu) — the anticipated Shekinah: the kavod (glory, divine radiant presence) that departed from the temple in Ezekiel's vision (Ezek 10:18; 11:23) will return and dwell (shakhan) in the land again. Ezek 43:1-5 describes the return of the kavod; Ps 85 prays for it.
10: 'Steadfast love and faithfulness meet; righteousness and peace kiss each other' — the most celebrated verse of the psalm, one of the most beautiful in the Psalter. Four covenant attributes personified: chesed (steadfast love) and emet (faithfulness/truth) meet (niphgashu); tzedek (righteousness) and shalom (peace) kiss (nashaquu). In the present age these attributes are in tension: the demands of righteousness and the gift of peace; the expectations of faithfulness and the need for love. The psalmist envisions a future moment when they converge. Medieval Christian commentators saw this as a prophecy of the incarnation: God's justice and mercy reconciled in Christ. The verse has served in Christian preaching as a summary of the atonement.
11: 'Faithfulness springs up from the earth, and righteousness looks down from the sky' — the cosmic dimensions of the restoration: emet (faithfulness) emerges from the earth (mitaretz titzmach, springs up like a plant) while tzedek (righteousness) looks down from the sky (min-shamayim nishqaph). Heaven and earth participate in the restoration: the created order aligns with the covenant attributes. This mirrors the creation-restoration language of Isa 45:8 ('let the skies rain down righteousness; let the earth open and salvation and righteousness spring up together').
12: 'Yes, the LORD will give what is good, and our land will yield its increase' — the covenant blessing formula: YHWH gives the good (hayyov) and the land yields its produce (artzenu titen yevulah). Lev 26:4 and Deut 28:12 include agricultural abundance as the primary covenant blessing. The post-exilic community, farming on land depleted by the exile and return, receives the promise that YHWH's restoration includes material, agricultural flourishing.
13: 'Righteousness will go before him and make his footsteps a way' — tzedek lefanav yehallech (righteousness will walk before him) — righteousness personified as YHWH's advance-herald, the forerunner preparing the way. The closing verse envisions YHWH's approach as a royal procession: righteousness goes ahead, then YHWH walks, and his footsteps become the path that others follow. The herald-before-the-king imagery anticipates Isa 40:3 ('prepare the way of the LORD') and the NT's application of that text to John the Baptist.
